_note: Arts 90 (Introduction), 100 (Definitions), 110 (Requirements for Electrical Installations). All small enough to live inline; no per-article files needed.

articles.scope: Defines the purpose, scope, arrangement and enforcement of the NEC. Sets out what the NEC covers (90.2(A)) and does not cover (90.2(B)). Establishes the AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) as the enforcement authority.

key_sections.title: Purpose
key_sections.summary: Practical safeguarding of persons and property from hazards arising from the use of electricity

key_sections.title: Scope
key_sections.summary: What NEC covers (and does NOT cover — utilities under their own jurisdiction, mining, ships, railway rolling stock, etc.)

key_sections.title: Code Arrangement
key_sections.summary: Chapters 1-4 apply generally; Chapters 5-7 supplement or modify; Chapter 8 stands alone; Chapter 9 informational

key_sections.title: Enforcement
key_sections.summary: AHJ has authority to interpret, grant special permission, waive specific requirements

key_sections.title: Mandatory Rules, Permissive Rules, and Explanatory Material
key_sections.summary: 'shall' = mandatory; 'shall be permitted' = permissive; Informational Notes are not enforceable

common_errors: Treating Informational Notes as enforceable — they are explanatory, not mandatory
Assuming utility-owned equipment falls under NEC — Section 90.2(B)(5) excludes installations under exclusive utility control
Forgetting that AHJ acceptance (90.4) is separate from NEC compliance — both are required
articles.divergence_notes: NEC's AHJ concept has no direct IEC equivalent — IEC relies on national adoption and certification regimes. NEC 90.3 chapter-arrangement rule (Chapters 5-7 supplement Chapters 1-4) has no IEC analogue; IEC parts each carry independent scope.
articles.usage_notes: Read 90.2 first on any new project to confirm NEC applies. AHJ interpretations vary — document the AHJ name, the contact, and any project-specific waivers in the design narrative.

articles.scope: Defines terms used throughout the NEC. Definitions in Article 100 apply globally; definitions in Part II apply to circuits operating over 1000 V AC. Article-specific definitions in individual articles override Article 100 within those articles.

key_sections.title: General
key_sections.summary: Definitions applicable to all NEC articles. Includes ampacity, branch circuit, feeder, service, grounded conductor, neutral conductor, equipment grounding conductor, etc.

key_sections.title: Over 1000 V AC, Nominal
key_sections.summary: Definitions applicable only to circuits operating over 1000 V AC

key_definitions.title: Critical NEC↔IEC terminology pairings (selected — full list in terminology.md)

rows.definition: Maximum current, in amperes, a conductor can carry continuously under conditions of use without exceeding its temperature rating
Circuit conductors between final OCPD and outlet(s)
Circuit conductors between service equipment and final branch-circuit OCPD
Conductors and equipment connecting the utility supply to the wiring system
System or circuit conductor intentionally grounded
Conductor connected to the neutral point of a system that is intended to carry current under normal conditions
Conductive path(s) that provides a ground-fault current path and connects normally non-current-carrying metal parts of equipment together and to the system grounded conductor or to the GEC
Conductor used to connect the system grounded conductor or the equipment to a grounding electrode
rows.iec_analogue: Main earthing conductor (no exact IEC term)

rows.definition: Connection between the grounded service conductor and EGC at the service
Clear space required in front of equipment likely to require examination, servicing, or maintenance while energized (110.26)

common_errors: Using NEC 'ground' interchangeably with IEC 'earth' in mixed-jurisdiction documents — confuses inspectors
Mixing 'grounded conductor' (load-carrying neutral that happens to be grounded) with 'grounding electrode conductor' (carries fault current only)
Treating service conductors as branch circuits — they are governed by Article 230 separately
articles.divergence_notes: Major terminology divergence — see nec-vs-iec-comparison.md and terminology.md for the full catalogue. Most-cited: grounded conductor (NEC) ≠ neutral conductor (IEC), but NEC also uses both terms; EGC (NEC) ≈ CPC (IEC); ampacity (NEC) ≈ current-carrying capacity Iz (IEC); branch circuit (NEC) = final circuit (IEC).
articles.usage_notes: Every cross-jurisdiction project should include a one-page terminology key. Use NEC terms in NEC-jurisdiction documents and IEC terms in IEC-jurisdiction documents; do NOT mix.

articles.scope: General requirements for the examination and approval, installation and use, access to and spaces about electrical equipment.

key_sections.title: Examination, Identification, Installation, Use, and Listing of Equipment
key_sections.summary: Equipment must be evaluated, identified, listed where required, and installed in accordance with listing

key_sections.title: Circuit Impedance, Short-Circuit Current Ratings, and Other Characteristics
key_sections.summary: OCPDs, conductors, and equipment must have short-circuit current ratings (SCCR) ≥ available fault current

key_sections.title: Temperature Limitations (the '60/75/90 °C rule')
key_sections.summary: Conductor ampacity limited to the lowest temperature rating of any connected terminal — typically 60 °C or 75 °C even when conductor is 90 °C rated

key_sections.title: Spaces About Electrical Equipment
key_sections.summary: Working clearances — depth (Table 110.26(A)(1)) × width (30 in min or width of equipment) × headroom (6.5 ft min) for equipment likely to be examined energised

key_sections.title: Working Space and Guarding (Over 1000 V)
key_sections.summary: Increased working clearances for HV equipment

Table_110.26(A)(1).title: Working Space Depth — to Live Parts (≤ 1000 V Nominal)
Table_110.26(A)(1)._conditions: Condition 1: exposed live parts on one side only, no live or grounded parts on the other. Condition 2: exposed live parts on one side, grounded parts on the other. Condition 3: exposed live parts on both sides.

common_errors: Ignoring 110.14(C) — using 90 °C ampacity column when terminals are 60 °C or 75 °C rated; ampacity must be limited to the lower
Specifying inadequate working clearance — Table 110.26(A)(1) is by voltage AND condition AND nominal voltage to ground (not phase-to-phase)
Forgetting 110.10 SCCR coordination — equipment marked 5 kA SCCR in a 22 kA available fault location is non-compliant
Confusing working space (110.26) with dedicated electrical space (110.26(E)) — both are required and have different criteria
related_iec_60364: IEC 60364-7-729 (Access to electrical installations and equipment)
articles.divergence_notes: NEC's 110.14(C) 60/75/90 °C termination rule is much more prescriptive than IEC's tabulated ratings — IEC ampacity tables (Annex B) generally assume terminations rated at the same temperature as the conductor. NEC 110.26 working space requirements are stated in feet/inches and structured by voltage-to-ground × condition; IEC 60364-7-729 uses metric and is generally less prescriptive. NEC has explicit SCCR coordination (110.10); IEC handles via Icc/Icu separately per device.
articles.usage_notes: 110.14(C) is the most common cause of failed inspections for first-time NEC designers. Always state the termination temperature rating on the schedule (60 °C, 75 °C, 90 °C) and confirm the conductor ampacity column matches.
